input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical system under study is a serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes, comprising components such as motors, gearboxes, and safety cells. Its operational domain is machining, characterized by high repeatability and accuracy requirements suitable for digital twinning.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides live-data linking for real-time monitoring, dashboard visualization for situational awareness, and friction model identification to improve robot motion accuracy through control capabilities in machining operations.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin implementation leverages Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies including SysML for system modeling, RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT for hierarchical abstraction levels. Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs are employed to define components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition. Behavior modeling is achieved through MontiArc Automaton and SysML v1.7 state machines, integrated with simulation tools like MathWorks Simulink, other simulation frameworks, and general-purpose programming languages including C++ and Java for detailed system behavior specification.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system's constellation is structured according to the Digital Shadow Reference Model (Fig. 1), comprising four core components: Asset (physical system), DataTrace (captured sensor data), Model (digital representation), and Processing (data interpretation module). DataTraces are aggregated from the Asset and processed by the Processing component using the Model to derive insights, maintaining a strictly one-way communication flow from physical to digital without autonomous feedback mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The computational models include functional and logical architectures modeled using SysML IBDs and ADLs such as AADL and MontiArc, with behavioral aspects described via state machines (SysML v1.7) and MontiArc Automaton. Analytical models are implemented using MathWorks Simulink and other simulation tools. Data sources originate from physical systems via one-way data flow as part of a digital shadow, with data management involving integration of heterogeneous modeling notations, tools, and stakeholder perspectives across multiple abstraction levels (requirements, functional, logical, product/technical layers). Model relationships are defined through component & connector structures specifying interfaces and interconnections for system composition.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data flows from physical to virtual utilize MQTT protocol for streaming sensor time series data, including input current and output angle. Preprocessing steps derive torque and angular velocity from raw sensor inputs. The system follows a one-way data flow characteristic of a digital shadow, with no feedback mechanisms to the physical system.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The system is a digital shadow with a strictly one-way data flow from physical to virtual; there is no virtual-to-physical interaction as the digital shadow functions as a passive entity and does not push data back to the physical system.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Technical connection employs MQTT protocol for real-time sensor data streaming and integrates Asset Administration Shells (AAS) for live-data linking. Specific details on network topology, bandwidth, latency, reliability, redundancy, or security protocols are not provided in the document.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Compliance with SysML for systems engineering modeling, ACME, AADL, and MontiArc as architecture description languages (ADLs) for component & connector perspectives, and methodologies including RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT for managing system complexity across abstraction levels.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the system as the 'serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes' and lists its components ('motor', 'gearbox', 'safety cell') as described in the text. It correctly identifies the operational domain as 'machining' and references the specific 'high path repeatability' and 'positional accuracy' figures provided in the source.

The extracted services are well-supported by the text. 'Live-data linking' and 'visualization in a dashboard' are explicitly mentioned in the Abstract and Section IV. 'Friction model identification' is identified as the core purpose in Section IV, with the goal to 'improve the robot’s motion accuracy' via 'model-based control algorithms' mentioned in the Introduction.

The extraction accurately lists MBSE methodologies, ADLs, and simulation tools found in Section II.A ('Modeling Software and Systems'). It correctly cites SysML, RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT, ACME, AADL, MontiArc, MathWorks Simulink, and general-purpose languages C++ and Java as tools used for modeling structure, behavior, and simulation.

The document focuses on a single digital shadow for one robot axis. It does not mention multiple digital twin instances, hierarchical structures, or coordination between twins. Therefore, 'Not in Document' is correct.

The extraction accurately summarizes the 'Digital Shadow Reference Model' described in the text, identifying Asset, DataTrace, Model, and Processing components. It correctly notes the 'automated one-way data flow' (Section II.B) and the role of the Processing component. It is specific to the system and technically grounded, though it simplifies the potential for 'active components' mentioned in the broader Digital Twin context.

The document describes integration with the physical robot (vertical integration) via MQTT and mentions Asset Administration Shells (AAS). However, it does not describe 'horizontal integration' patterns with other Digital Twins or enterprise systems (e.g., ERP, MES) as implied by the characteristic definition. Thus, 'Not in Document' is correct.
